coredata.dc:title: Multifunctional performance of chitosan/soy protein isolation-based films impregnated carbon dots/anthocyanin derived from purple hull pistachio for tracking and extending the shelf life of fish

coredata.dc:description: The present study aimed to develop a sustainable biopolymer film that exhibits active and intelligent properties. The chitosan/soy protein isolation (Cs/SPI) based film was prepared by blending purple hull pistachio anthocyanin (PHPA) with carbon dots (CDs), which were synthesized using a by-product from purple hull pistachio (PHP) extraction. PHPA and CDs were shown to be compatible with the Cs/SPI biopolymer films. The resulting matrix was a homogeneous film with improved physico-mechanical properties. The biopolymer films were characterized by their various desirable properties, including 100% UV protection, potent antibacterial activity against S.aureus and E. coli strains with inhibition zonoe about 22.1 ± 36±0.24 mm and 20.36 ± 0.21 mm, respectively, and strong antioxidant properties (DPPH; 82.3 ± 0.1 % and ABTS; 90.6 ± 0.1 %). The film was also found to exhibit distinguishable colorimetric responses to pH 2–12 buffers and volatile ammonia. The packaging test of CS/SPI/PHPA/PHP-CDs film in scenario one (intelligent assay) for evaluation through fish freshness monitoring showed that the film's color changed from light pink (fresh fish) to yellow/brownish (spoilage fish) during storage at 4 °C and 25 °C. Also, scenario two (active assay) at 4 °C showed the ability to increase the shelf life of fish up to 12 days. The study demonstrated that the biopolymer films have exhibited exceptional adaptability, making it a highly promising option for prompt and effective on-site detection of food quality. Furthermore, the film's capacity to prolong the shelf life of fish and reduce the rate of quality degradation during storage positions it as an outstanding contender for multifunctional film applications in the food industry.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The present study aimed to develop a sustainable biopolymer film that exhibits active and intelligent properties. The chitosan/soy protein isolation (Cs/SPI) based film was prepared by blending purple hull pistachio anthocyanin (PHPA) with carbon dots (CDs), which were synthesized using a by-product from purple hull pistachio (PHP) extraction. PHPA and CDs were shown to be compatible with the Cs/SPI biopolymer films. The resulting matrix was a homogeneous film with improved physico-mechanical properties. The biopolymer films were characterized by their various desirable properties, including 100% UV protection, potent antibacterial activity against S.aureus and E. coli strains with inhibition zonoe about 22.1 ± 36±0.24 mm and 20.36 ± 0.21 mm, respectively, and strong antioxidant properties (DPPH; 82.3 ± 0.1 % and ABTS; 90.6 ± 0.1 %). The film was also found to exhibit distinguishable colorimetric responses to pH 2–12 buffers and volatile ammonia. The packaging test of CS/SPI/PHPA/PHP-CDs film in scenario one (intelligent assay) for evaluation through fish freshness monitoring showed that the film's color changed from light pink (fresh fish) to yellow/brownish (spoilage fish) during storage at 4 °C and 25 °C. Also, scenario two (active assay) at 4 °C showed the ability to increase the shelf life of fish up to 12 days. The study demonstrated that the biopolymer films have exhibited exceptional adaptability, making it a highly promising option for prompt and effective on-site detection of food quality. Furthermore, the film's capacity to prolong the shelf life of fish and reduce the rate of quality degradation during storage positions it as an outstanding contender for multifunctional film applications in the food industry.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Many food packaging materials are made from oil-based plastics, which can have harmful effects on the environment and can transfer microplastics to food, increasing the risk of cancer in humans. To address these concerns, using biodegradable, non-toxic, and environmentally friendly packaging made from proteins, polysaccharides, and other natural materials is a viable solution (Bangar, Whiteside, Kajla, & Tavassoli, 2023; Tavassoli, 2024). As such, the development of food packaging films based on biopolymers (e.g. polysaccharides, proteins, gums, and their blend) has received increasing attention in recent years. These biodegradable films are biodegradable and renewable, which is advantageous to environmental protection (Xu et al., 2024). Biodegradable films based on macromolecules or their derivatives are such that the body can absorb and digest them. These compounds create a network structure through electrostatic interactions, hydrogen bonding, and molecular entanglement, giving the film particular barrier and mechanical properties (Oladzadabbasabadi, Abraham, Ghasemlou, Ivanova, & Adhikari, 2024).

Biodegradable films that combine polysaccharides and proteins increase the properties of the intrinsic polyelectrolyte and functional chemical groups, which in turn leads to an increase in the properties of the film (Tavassoli, Khezerlou, Firoozy, Ehsani, & Punia Bangar, 2023). Chitosan (CS) has been used as a polysaccharide polymer in biodegradable packaging due to its biodegradability, cost-effectiveness, biocompatibility, non-toxicity, and ability to prepare films. This polymer has a linear structure of (1–4)-2-acetamido-2-deoxy-β-D-glucan and (1–4)-2-amino-2-deoxy-β-D-glucan. Hydroxyl and amino CS groups can increase the film's strength by creating hydrogen bonds (Parveen et al., 2024). However, pure CS film has disadvantages such as weak mechanical strength, low selectivity, and high swelling index in water, which limits its use in food packaging. But in the structure of CS, there are amino groups, which makes it prone to chemical changes to overcome these disadvantages. Soy protein isolate (SPI), obtained from oil processing waste, has been used in film production due to its cheapness and high protein content. SPI contain glycine and beta-conglycinin, which are stable because of the hydrogen bond, disulfide bond, and hydrophobic interaction in the structure of glycine. Therefore, this composition's ionic and hydrogen bonds cause film forming properties (Qi et al., 2024). However, SPI film has problems such as poor stability, but its flexibility for the chemical reaction of the amino acid structure in protein macromolecules has made it possible to develop a biodegradable film (Zeng, Gao, Zhu, He, & Yu, 2023). It has been reported that using natural dyes and nanomaterials in biodegradable packaging can overcome the disadvantages of poor mechanical properties and film barriers (Riahi, Khan, Rhim, Shin, & Kim, 2024a). In recent years, nanomaterials based on carbon dots (CDs) have gained significant attention as eco-friendly alternatives for modifying the physicochemical and barrier properties of films.

The CDs have been used as active fillers in biodegradable, edible active films to meet the requirements of food safety. CDs are a carbon-based nanomaterial less than 10 nm in size, with a zero-dimensional structure. They are cost-effective, low toxicity, and biocompatible. In addition, functional groups in the structure of CDs show useful applications, such as antioxidant, antimicrobial, and ultraviolet-ray blocking (Riahi et al., 2024a). Also, they have properties such as adjustable luminescence, light-induced electron transfer, photocatalytic processes, and good chemical stability. Compared to other carbon-based nanomaterials, CDs are soluble in water and have more modification capabilities. By adding CDs, the physicochemical properties of the films (mechanical resistance, gas barrier, and blocking UV light) have been improved. Previous studies have tested CDs as an activator and connectors in intelligent systems to monitor food freshness. Food waste is one of the best sources for the production of CDs because it reduces environmental pollution and brings economic benefits (Riahi et al., 2024a; Thirumalaivasan et al., 2024). Until now, Kohlrabi peel, banana peel, and citrus peel wastes have been used to synthesize and prepare CDs (Gong et al., 2023; Riahi et al., 2024a; Sul, Ezati, & Rhim, 2023). Pistachio nuts are incredibly valuable due to their abundance of phenolic compounds, making them an outstanding functional food. Pistachio external skins are industrially separated from the nuts during processes and account for approximately 10% of the total weight of shelled pistachios. If left unprocessed, these become waste and can contribute to environmental pollution when released without proper control measures. Consequently, there is a keen interest in valorizing pistachio by-products (Alalwan et al., 2022). In this study, CDs obtaine from purple hull pistachio (PHP) and incorporated into active CS/SPI-based films. However, the active packaging systems generally lack real-time freshness/spoilage tracking, hindering consumer selection based on desired quality. As such, the demand is rising for multifunctional intelligent active packaging systems to both maintain food freshness and provide timely monitoring. Accordingly, the quick and releable tracking of food quality status has greatly enhanced the attractiveness of intelligent active packaging systems utilizing pH-sensitive color-changing dyes (Tavassoli, Khezerlou, Moghaddam, et al., 2023; Yekta, Abedi-Firoozjah, Azimi Salim, Khezerlou, & Abdolmaleki, 2023). Anthocyanins, as a natural colorant, among the different value-added substances obtained from agri-food by-products, have gained a surge of unprecedented interest from researchers and industries, due to their technological and biological properties (Arruda et al., 2021; Tomaino et al., 2010). Also, the halochromic behavior of anthocyanins allow them to change color in response to different external conditions like changes in pH level, gas accumulation, and temperature fluctuation. As such, the utilization of outer pistachio skins as an alternative source of biologically bioactive and intelligent substances can holds great potential in the smart food packaging industries (Arruda et al., 2021; Bhargava, Sharanagat, Mor, & Kumar, 2020; Tomaino et al., 2010). Recycling anthocyanins and carbon dots from pistachio by-products can create economic value by developing new products and reducing waste. Not only does this improve the sustainability of packaging materials, but it also opens up new market opportunities (Xu et al., 2024). More so, it can further the several Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs). As such, this approach supports SDG 12 by reducing food waste and improving a circular economy. It not only fuels innovation in the food industry (SDG 9), but also enhances public health with beneficial compounds like anthocyanins (SDG 3), while minimizing the carbon footprint to advance climate action (SDG 13) (Kumar Gupta et al., 2024; Matthes & Schmid, 2024).

This study aimed to develop a new sustainable colorimetric functional film with the potential for food preservation by combining biowaste-derived CDs and anthocyanin with CS-based film. To this end, a CS/SPI-based multifunctional composite film was developed using anthocyanin extracted from the purple hull pistachio (PHPA) and CDs derived from biowaste (PHP-CDs). In addition, the ammonia test and pH-sensitive color change of the films were analyzed. The films' pH-sensitive color change and ammonia test results were also examined. Finally, the designed films were characterized and evaluated for their functional capabilities as tracking freshness and increasing the shelf life of fish samples.

CS with a purity of more than 99%, size 20–50 nm, degree of acetylation 80–85%, M.W. 50–80 kDa) was obtained from Nano Novin Polymer Company (Mazandaran, Iran). Nutrient broth and Mueller Hinton agar were prepared by Quelab (Montreal, Canada). E. coli and S. aureus strains were obtained from the Center for Biological and Genetic Resources (Tehran, Iran). All prepared chemicals were used without further purification and were of analytical grade. SPI (protein content ≥98%) was obtained from Shandong Yuwang Biotechnology Co., Ltd., Dezhou, China. Glycerol and ethanol were purchased from Kimia Shimi in Tabriz. Fresh purple hull pistachio (Pistacia vera) was purchased from Rafsanjan City, Kerman, Iran.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Extraction of anthocyanin and synthesis of CDs from pistachio skin
ce:section.ce:para: Anthocyanin and CDs were obtained from fresh purple hull pistachio (see Scheme 1). The extraction of anthocyanin in this work was done according to the previous studies done by these authors (Tavassoli, Khezerlou, Bakhshizadeh, et al., 2024). To extract anthocyanin, pistachio skin was dried and turned into powder. 5g of the obtained powder was added to 100 ml of solvent (distilled water/ethanol; 80/20 V/V) and kept at 25 °C for 24 h. It was stirred. Then the obtained solution was filtered through Whatman filter paper (No. 1). The obtained extract was concentrated using a rotary operator at a temperature of 37 °C, and finally the total anthocyanins content of PSPA (based on the cyanidin-3 mg) were determined by Kan et al. (2022) (Kan et al., 2022). Accordingly, the extracts were dissolved in buffer solutions at pH 1.0 and pH 4.5. These solutions were then scanned at 520 nm and 700 nm, respectively, using a UV–vis spectrophotometer. The total anthocyanin content was determined based on the changes in absorption at both pH levels. For CDs synthesis, pistachio skin has been used by a one-step hydrothermal method. In short, 1g of pistachio powder was dispersed in 50 ml of deionized water with stirring and autoclaved (8 h at 190 °C). The obtained product was centrifuged (at a speed of 1000 rpm, for 30 min) and the supernatant was filtered using Whatman paper to remove impurities.

Biopolymer films were produced using the solvent casting method with chitosan (CS) and soy protein isolate (SPI) as the raw materials. A 3% w/v CS solution was created by dissolving CS in 100 mL of 1% acetic acid and stirred at room temperature for 5 h to yield mixture A. SPI (3 g) dissolved in 100 mL of distilled water with continuous stirring preparing mixture B. Subsequently, the obtained mixture B and 1.8 g of glycerol (as a plasticizer; 30 wt% based on polymer) were added into the mixture A via mild stirring for 1.5 h to completely mix. Further, three different functional film solutions of CS/SPI/PHP-CDs, CS/SPI/PHPA, and CS/SPI/PHPA/PHP-CDs were prepared by dissolving 2 % (w/w) CD, 3% (w/v) PHPA, and 2 % (w/w) CD + 3% (w/v) PHPA, respectively, to the CS/SPI film solution following the same procedure (Table 1 ). The pure CS/SPI was also selected as control film. Finally, the resulting solution was poured onto a glass plate (diameter; 30 cm) and placed in an oven at 40 °C for 24 h to form the film. Once the films were prepared, they were stored at 25 °C and 50% relative humidity.

To confirm the use of PHPA as a pH-sensitive dyes, the color change of this solution was assessed at different pH values (1-14), then their images were taken and their optical absorption was recorded by a spectrophotometer (Ultrospec 2000; Scinteck, UK) ranging from 400 to 800 nm (Tavassoli, Khezerlou, Bakhshizadeh, et al., 2024). Elemental composition and the functional groups PHP-CDs were assessed through an analysis using a Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR; Shimadzu Co., Japan) in the wavelength range of 4000–500 cm−1. Light absorption and fluorescence responses was measured using a Uv–vis spectrophotometer and a fluorescence spectrophotometer, respectively. DLS test has also been done to measure the size of CDs. The antioxidant potential of the PHP-CDs and CS-based films was evaluated by ABTS•+ and DPPH• scavenging techniques using a UV–visible spectrophotometer at 734 nm and 517 nm, respectively, based on the following formul (Riahi, Khan, Rhim, Shin, & Kim, 2024b):
R a d i c a l

r a t e
( % )

)

. Where, A1 is the absorbance of the control, and A2 is the absorbance of the test samples. The antibacterial efficacy of the CDs and developed films was also determined using the agar well diffusion technique (10 mm diameter wells) against foodborne pathogenic bacteria E. coli and S. aureus, as mentioned in our previous study (Tavassoli, Khezerlou, Bakhshizadeh, et al., 2024).

To examine the surface morphology of the films, a SEM (TESCAN Mira 3, Czech Republic) was utilized. Initially, the films underwent a gold coating process (DST1, Nanostructured Coating Co., Tehran, Iran) within an argon gas environment. Data on the molecular interactions within the composite films was gathered utilizing Attenuated Total Reflectance Fourier Transform Infrared (ATR-FTIR; Bruker, Germany) spectroscopy, which analyzed the IR spectrum ranging from 4000 to 400 cm−1. Details regarding the crystallographic characteristics of the films were acquired through X-ray diffraction (XRD; Philips, Amsterdam, Netherlands) analysis. XRD patterns of the film samples were obtained by exposing them to a Ni-filtered Cu Kα radiation beam (40 kV and 30 mA) and measuring signal intensity within the angular range (2θ) from 5 to 80°. Thermal behavior of CS-based composites films were determined by thermogravimetric analyser (TGA/DTG; TA Instrument, New Castle, DE, USA) by scanning rate of 10 °C/min from 10 to 700 °C in nitrogen atmosphere. The water contact angle (WCA) was determined using a contact angle analyzer (Phoenix 150, Republic of Korea). The sample size used was 3 cm × 6 cm, with water as the test liquid. A drop size of 5 μL was applied, and the equilibrium time for measurement was set to 20 s.

Thickness, mechanical properties (TS; tensile strength and EAB; elongation at break), water solubility (WS), moisture content (MC), swelling index (SI), and water vapor permeability (WVP) of prepared CS-based films were determined based on our currently published methods (ASTM, 2010; Khezerlou, Alizadeh; Sani et al., 2023; Tavassoli, Khezerlou, Bakhshizadeh, et al., 2024). Optical properties, including transparency, light transmission, and UV–vis absorption, were measured using a spectrophotometer in the range of 200–800 nm. Transparency was calculated using the following formula (Bakhshizadeh, Moghaddam, Tavassoli, Ayaseh, & Bangar, 2023):

The color coordinates of prepared CS-based films (L∗, a∗, b∗) were determined using a Hunter lab colorimeter (CR-400, Minolta, Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH and ammonia sensitivity of CS-based films
ce:section.ce:para:
To assess the pH-sensitivity of the synthesized anthocyanin-rich CS films, the samples were immersed in buffer solutions with different pH values (2-12) and then the color change of the films was recorded using digital photography. Further, the ability of films to sense ammonia vapors was evaluated based on our previously adopted research (Khezerlou et al., 2023). Briefly, films were cut into discs with a diameter of 2 cm and placed 1 cm above a dish containing ammonia solution (8 mM) at 25 °C for 30 min. During this period, every 5 min, the color change of the films was photographed. Then, based on the observed changes in color, the sensitivity of the film to ammonia vapor was calculated as follows:
S
R G B
( % )
=
(
R i − R f
)
+
(
G i − G f
)
+
(
B i − B f
)
R i + G i + B i
× 100
Here, Ri, Gi, Bi, Rf, Gf, and Bf represent the initial and final red, green, and blue values of the films, respectively. These values were measured using the Pixie program for Windows.

The antioxidant ability of the chitosan-based films was evaluated using DPPH• and ABTS•+ scavenging methods, as described by Riahi et al. (2024) (Riahi et al., 2024b). The antibacterial properties of the prepared films were assessed using the disc diffusion method against S. aureus and E. coli as examples of Gram-positive pathogens and Gram-negative pathogens, respectively. Accordingly, the films were cut into 1 × 1 cm dimensions and placed on the plate; then they were incubated at 37 °C for 24 h. The diameter of the inhibition zone around the film discs was measured with a Vernier caliper (Riahi et al., 2024a).

The release rate of anthocyanin from the synthesized films was evaluated by immersing them in 25 mL of ethanol solutions (10%, and 95%) simulating model food conditions (Tavassoli, Khezerlou, Bakhshizadeh, et al., 2024). Square pieces of the films (2 × 2 cm) were immersed in the food model at 25 °C and gently shaken for 4 h. The absorbance of the solutions was monitored over time (30, 60, 90, 120, 180, and 240 min) at a wavelength of 520 nm using a UV–visible spectrophotometer. The pigment release rate was then calculated as micrograms of PHPA released per square millimeter of film using a standard curve.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of CS-based film to monitor and increase shelf life of fish
ce:section.ce:para: Commercially available fish fillets were procured from local fishery market (Tabriz, Iran) and immediately transported to the laboratory under cold condition (4 °C) within 30 min. Then, two different test scenarios were performed to determine the films' performance.

In first scenario, fish slices were placed in a polyethylene plastic bags (PE) and a label specimen (CS/SPI/PHPA/PHP-CDs film; circular disk with a diameter of 2 cm) was attached to the inside of each container. Then, the PE bags were kept at 4 °C/12 days and 25 °C/8 days to test the color change of the CS/SPI/PHPA/PHP-CDs film at a 24-h interval during storage period. The apparent color changes of the prepared lable were tracked by the bare eye and recorded using digital photography. The color coordinates of lable (L∗, a∗, b∗) were also recorded using a Hunter lab colorimeter (CR-400, Minolta, Japan) (Sangeetha et al., 2024). Meanwhile, the microbial quality of packaged fillets was assessed via total viable count (TVC; plate count agar incubated at 37 ± 1 °C for 2 d) and psychrotrophic bacterial count (PTC; plate count agar incubated 7 ± 1 °C for 10 d) (Mohebi, Abbasvali, & Shahbazi, 2023). Three sets of experiments were done for each stage, and the outcomes were presented as log CFU/mL. The packaged samples were examined for chemical quality including Total volatile base nitrogen level (TVB-N; mg N/100 g, via the steam distillation method by a Kjeldahl distillation apparatus) (Cao et al., 2023), pH (by a digital pH meter (Thermo Scientific, Indonesia)) (Tavassoli, Khezerlou, Firoozy, et al., 2023). Oxidative degradation was also assessed in term of level of peroxide value (PV; meq peroxide/1000 g lipid) and thiobarbituric acid reactive substances (TBA; mg malondialdehyde (MDA)/kg lipid) based on the method previously reported by urgut et al. (2016) (Turgut, Soyer, & Işıkçı, 2016). All chemicals and culture media utilized in this research were obtained from Merck, Germany.

In second scenario, to assess the ability of prepared films to delay spoilage of fresh fish fillets stored at cold condition for 12 d, samples (10 ± 0.1 g) was placed into glass petri dishes and wrapped the with the film samples that were classified in three test groups, namely (i) untreated group (unwrapped dishes), (ii) control group (sample wrapped with neat CS/SPI film), and (iii) treated group (sample wrapped with CS/SPI/PHPA/PHP-CDs film). Sampling was done every 24 h during storage, and the fish quality analysis (TVC, PTC, pH, TVB-N, TBA, and PV) was performed in triplicate.

Fig. 1 a illustrates the color changes of PHPA at different pH levels ranging from pH 1 to 14. The PHPA indicated a light pink color below pH 3, shifted to red color within the pH range of 4–6, turned dark green at pH 7–9, changed to green grass at pH 9–10, and appeared greenish brown at 11–14. The obvious color change of PHPA could indeed be linked to the pH-induced changes in the chemical structure of anthocyanins, specifically malvin, delphinidin, and malvidin (Heydarian & Shavisi, 2023; Tavassoli, Khezerlou, Bakhshizadeh, et al., 2024). Here, the total anthocyanin content in PHPA was also found to be 1.359 ± 0.003 mg/mL of cyanidin-3-glucoside. Other anthocyanins-rich plant extracts, like Malva sylvestris (Shavisi, 2024), red poppy (Tavassoli, Khezerlou, Bakhshizadeh, et al., 2024), and black barberry (Heydarian et al., 2023), have also shown a similar color change across the different pH spectrum. Consistent with the color changes observed in the PHPA solution, the maximum absorption peaks in the solution also varied, as depicted in the UV–visible spectra (Fig. 1c). Under highly acidic conditions (pH 1–3), the spectrum of PHPA exhibited a unique absorption peak at 352 nm, which is ascribed to the red flavylium cation in the PHPA solution. As the pH gradually reduced from 3 to 6, the peak absorbance decreased and shifted from 480 nm to 600 nm. In line with the PHPA color changes at pH 7–9, the maximum absorption peak shifted to 456 nm while the absorbance decreased. This occurred because the quinonoidal anhydro-base is thought to be the primary component in the solution. Subsequently, a new peak emerged at approximately 528 nm, with its intensity and position being correlated to pH 9, a characteristic feature of anthocyanin. Additionally, the intensity of this peak decreased when the pH was raised from 10 to 14. In this stage, anthocyanin molecules degraded under the alkaline conditions to chalcone (opening ring structure), causing the color changed to light brown. These variations are likely due to alterations in the absorption spectrum and color of PHPA solutions within the molecular species present. In a study done by Heydarian and Shavisi (2023), the exposure of black barberry anthocyanins to strongly acidic conditions (red color, pH 1) and basic conditions (brown, pH 10–12) led to a shift of the maximum absorption peak from 520 nm to 611 nm (Heydarian et al., 2023).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Functionality and optical properties of PHP-CDs

ce:section.ce:para: Determining the capability of substances to eliminate or counteract free radicals is typically accomplished through assessing antioxidant activity. Fig. 2 A presents the antioxidant activity of PHP-CDs in terms of DPPH and ABTS scavenging analysis. Here, the DPPH and ABTS radical solution's color transition from deep violet and cyan to light yellow and colorless, attributed to the formation of the corresponding hydrazine DPPH-H and ABTS-H complexes, respectively (Khan, Ezati, & Rhim, 2023; Riahi et al., 2024a). The PHP-CDs indicated significant and content-dependent DPPH and ABTS radical scavenging activity. At a concentration of 100 μg/mL, PHP-CDs indicated around ∼35 % and ∼40 % of radical scavenging ability for DPPH and ABTS free radicals, respectively, and antioxidant activity gradually increased with enhancing concentration of CDs and finally reached to ∼80 % and ∼90 % at 500 μg/mL concentration, respectively. The pairing of a lone pair of electrons from the DPPH and ABTS with a cation or H+ from the PHP-CDs could explain this phenomenon. More so, the surface of PHP-CDs is rich in reducing groups like OH, COOH, and HS, which promote the reaction and reduction of free radicals. The radical scavenging activity is enhanced with an increase in the number of carboxyl and –OH groups on the surface of PHP-CDs. The presence of these groups allows for the transfer of H• from –OH of PHP-CDs to DPPH-H and ABTS-H, thereby reducing and quenching the radical scavenging effect. With a lone pair of electrons on an N atom and surrounded by three benzene rings, the DPPH and ABTS molecules are able to accept hydrogen shifts and engage in reactions with PHP-CDs. Thus, the DPPH and ABTS radicals can be neutralized by CDs through a combination of hydrogen donation, electron transfer, and radical adduct formation (Riahi et al., 2024a; Sul et al., 2023). The greater antioxidant potential of ABTS than DPPH was linked to the hydrophilic properties of PHP-CDs, which enhanced the solubility of dots in the aqueous ABTS solution compared to the methanolic DPPH solution (Riahi et al., 2024a; Sul et al., 2023). The substantial radical neutralizing potential of CD as an active filler in food packaging system has been previously studied (Riahi et al., 2024a; Sul et al., 2023; Wagh, Khan, Priyadarshi, Ezati, & Rhim, 2023).

In Fig. 2B(a-e), the antibacterial effectiveness of PHPA/CDs and PHP-CDs solutions against Gram-negative (E. coli) and Gram-positive (S. aureus) bacteria is shown. The PHPA/CDs solutions resulted in inhibition zones ranging from 23.01 ± 0.2 mm for S. aureus to 21.17 ± 0.6 mm for E. coli. Similarly, the PHP-CDs solutions produced inhibition zones ranging from 21.03 ± 0.3 mm for S. aureus to 19.89 ± 0.2 mm for E. coli (Fig. 2Ba). The findings indicate that the antibacterial impact of PHPA/CD exceeds that of PHP-CD. This suggests that the presence of anthocyanin, potentially attributable to the high levels of antimicrobial phenolic groups in the anthocyanin molecules, functions synergistically with CD in suppressing bacterial growth. It was also shown that S. aureus is more sensitive than E.coli. This sensitivity is reflected in a broader inhibition zone, primarily attributed to differences in cell wall makeup and the binding affinity of surface functional groups of CDs to bacterial cell membrane. The presence of a single peptidoglycan layer and exposed peptide sequences in the cell wall of Gram-positive bacteria plays a vital role in promoting a stronger bond with CDs. This facilitate easier entry of the CDs into the interior of the bacterial cell. On the contrary, the thick, double-layered peptidoglycan cell walls characteristic of Gram-negative bacteria present obstacles in their interactions with dots and makes them more stable against the damaging effects of reactive oxygen species (ROS). The precise mechanism behind the antibacterial activity of CDs remains to be fully understood, but previous studies have revealed that CDs exert their antibacterial effects by binding to the surface of bacterial cells. This binding initiates a cascade of reactions that result in the production of ROS, disrupting the antioxidant and oxidative balance within the cell. The resulting imbalance in antioxidant and oxidative processes effectively leads to the degradation of biomolecules in the cell wall, ultimately causing cell destruction. Due to their small size and large surface area, CDs are effective at making contact with microorganisms, which enhances their ability to kill them. CDs can also physically interact with microbial cell membranes, disrupting and causing leakage of intracellular components. This ultimately leads to cell death and inhibits microbial growth (Ahmadi et al., 2024; Oladzadabbasabadi et al., 2023). This result is consistent with the results obtained for various types of CD (Khan et al., 2023; Riahi et al., 2024a).

The synthesized PHP-CDs have outstanding photon absorption properties, particularly in the short wavelength regions, as indicated by the analysis using UV–Vis and fluorescence spectrophotometry. The fluorescence of PHP-CDs shows strong excitation intensity at 370 nm and emission intensity at 484 nm (Fig. 2D), primarily due to electron transfers involving π → π∗ bonds of C-C and n → π∗ bonds of C-C. The UV light absorption by PHP-CDs suggests their potential suitability for the development of UV barrier active packaging films (Fig. 2E). Also, the DLS results indicated that the size of the CDs is approximately 4 nm, which is ideal for their application in packaging films (Fig. 2F). The fluorescence spectrum of CDs demonstrated the most pronounced fluorescence emission at 484 nm when excited at a wavelength of 370 nm. Moreover, the fluorescence emission exhibited a redshift with increasing excitation wavelength (Fig. 2C), signifying the excitation-dependent nature of CDs' emission. When subjected to UV light (365 nm), the CD solution displayed a vivid blue scattering emission, while appearing completely transparent under normal light (Fig. 2E). It is firmly established that CDs act as potent photon emitters when excited by UV light. Similar results of this phenomenon were demonstrated in a study by Wagh et al. (2023) on carbon dots synthesized from Brassica oleracea waste materials (Wagh et al., 2023).

FTIR spectra were performed to determine the changes of the composition and functional groups in CS-based film. As illustrates in Fig. 3 A, all CS-based films had similar spectra. Bands observed at 2920, 2645 cm−1 and 3416 cm−1 interval in the chitosan spectrum can stem from the stretching vibration of -CH, -NH, and OH groups, as well as inter/intra-molecular hydrogen bonding (K. Li et al., 2017). The peaks detected at ∼ 1689 and 1408 cm−1 were indicated to the amide I (C–O stretching), amide II (N–H bending), and amide III (C–H and N–H stretching), whereas the peaks at 1597, 1421, 1319, 1258, 1160, 1079, 1029, and 894 cm−1 could be attributed to the N–H of NH2, CH2 bending, O–H bending, asymmetric bridge oxygen (C–O–C) stretching, –C–O–C– in glycosidic linkage, and glucose ring stretching of CS, respectively (Khan et al., 2023; Sul et al., 2023). In the CS/SPI composite films, characteristic bands were observed at 1675 cm⁻1 and 1394 cm⁻1, identified as amide I and II, respectively. The Amide I band may consist of multiple overlapping components due to the presence of various protein segments with differing secondary structures (K. Li et al., 2017; Sul et al., 2023).

The peak observed around 3274 cm−1 was attributable to stretch vibration absorption peak of O–H, exhibited a noticeable downward shift in comparison to the CS spectra, which is likely attributed to the formation of hydrogen bonds resulting from the incorporation of protein. The typical characteristic bands of pure CS film at 1060 cm−1 assigned to glycosidic linkage (–C–O–C–), observed a small displacement to lower wavenumbers after the incorporation of SPI (Yong et al., 2023; Zareie, Yazdi, & Mortazavi, 2020). After adding SPI, the absorption peaks at 1058 cm−1 and 1063 cm−1, associated with C–O stretching, merged to form a single peak, showing that hydrogen bonding interactions took place between the hydroxyl groups of CS and SPI (K. Li et al., 2017; Zareie et al., 2020).

The CS/SPI film loaded with PHPA and/or PHP-CDs exhibited FTIR patterns akin to the pure CS/SPI film, with only a minor variance in peak intensity, which is closely attributed to physical interactions (hydrogen bond and van der Waals) and non-covalent bonds between the functional groups of the PHPA and/or PHP-CDs and the polymer matrix. In this respect, the peak around 3426 cm−1 broadened as a result of the synergistic stretching of hydrogen-bonded N–H groups (Wagh et al., 2023). More so, The negligible change observed in the FTIR spectra may be ascribed to the similar elemental compositions and surface functionalities of the materials used, specifically chitosan, SPI, PHPA, and PHP-CDs. The stretching peaks of the aromatic rings (2900–2928 cm−1 and 3356–3498 cm−1), associated with anthocyanin, align with their presence in the CS/SPI/PHP-CD/PHPA films. These changes in the peak positions in the pure CS/SPI and CS/SPI/PHP-CD/PHPA films suggest that the O-H groups in anthocyanins formed hydrogen bonds with the N-H and carboxyl groups of the PHP-CD and polymer matrix (Khan et al., 2023; Riahi et al., 2024a). The similar FTIR spectra in all CS-based films could be due to the overlapping of stretching vibrations of functional groups like H–O, C–H, C=C, N–H, and C–S in the IR region, indicating the compatibility of PHP-CD/PHPA with the CS/SPI-polymer matrix (Riahi et al., 2024a). The FT-IR spectra of CDs derived from the PHP exhibit a significant presence of peaks found in the precursor material. The introduction of C=N bonds during the graphitization process likely leads to the emergence of a shoulder peak at 1389 cm−1, causing an overall broadening of the peak in that frequency range. Moreover, the observation of a new peak at 1698 cm−1 suggests the formation of fresh carbonyl groups during the hydrothermal reaction. The heightened peak intensity at 2996 cm−1 and 3423 cm−1 is due to the condensation of hydroxyl groups.

The density and hardness of a film typically increase as its level of crystallinity increases. To measure the crystallinity of the different CS-based films, X-ray diffraction was utilized (Fig. 3Ba). All films exhibited similar spectral patterns, with clear peaks at 2θ = ∼12° (associated with the diameter of the triple helix in CS) and 2θ = ∼18, 20, and 22° (associated with the distance between adjacent polypeptide strands of SPI). The functional groups of the CS-based films remained unaltered following the addition of PHPA and CDs, indicating strong compatibility between the CS-based films, PHPA, and CDs. The subtle changes in peak intensity and position are likely due to the abundant pore structure and functional groups present on the surface of CDs. CDs' uniform dispersion in composite films can be attributed to their amorphous and non-crystalline characteristics (S. Chen, Zeng, et al., 2023). Parveen et al. (2024) noted that while the crystal structure remained largely unchanged with the addition of CDs, increasing concentrations from 1% to 5% resulted in slightly wider diffraction peaks at around 20.3°–23.1° in chitosan/gelatin films. They observed that the presence of CDs was generally detectable by the widening of these XRD peaks (Parveen et al., 2024).

The microstructure and morphological characteristics of the CS/SPI-based films was examined using SEM, as depicted in Fig. 4 A. The neat CS film had a compact and smooth surface, without any voids or cracks. The surface of the pure CS/SPI film was smooth, intact and compact, free from voids and cracks. When SPI was introduced, the surface of the CS/SPI film maintained a smooth and intact surface, indicating that CS and SPI were blended in a compatible manner, leading to homogenously mixed CS/SPI films. The film loaded with 2 wt% PHP-CD displayed a slightly denser and smoother texture compared to the neat CS/SPI film, and it was still free of voids and cracks, showing good compatibility of CD with CS/SPI matrix and its uniform distribution in the CS/SPI casting solution at low content. It can be noted that the hydrophilicity of PHP-CDs and the creation of hydrogen bonds between the surface functional groups can be responsible for the uniform mixing of PHP-CDs and chitosan/SPI matrices (Wagh et al., 2023). However, upon the inclusion of 3%, v/v PHPA, the film surface exhibited subtle swelling and undulating irregularities attributed to the hydrophilic interaction of PHPA. After adding both PHPA and PHP-CD concurrently, the microstructure of the CS/SPI/PHPA/PHP-CD film gradually became smooth and more compact than the CS/SPI/PHPA film. It is possible to propose that the uniform blending of PHPA/PHP-CD and CS/SPI matrices may be ascribed to the hydrophilic nature of PHPA and PHP-CD. Indeed, improved uniformity and adhesion are becasue of the enhanced polarity of the aqueous extraction solvent from the extracted PHPA, along with the creation of electrostatic interactions and hydrogen bonds from the added PHP-CDs and chitosan/SPI biopolymers, leading to denser, cohesive films. Sul et al. (2023) and Wagh et al. (2023) found that by adding banana peel (Sul et al., 2023) and Brassica oleracea CDs (Wagh et al., 2023), the surface appearance of traded film remained uncompromised, with no presence of cracks, voids, or lumps, respectively, due to the good compatibility, even distribution, and embedding capabilities of CDs within the interspaces of the polymer matrices. Khan et al. (2024)also reported that after adding Kohlrabi peel extract and Zn-CDs into the carrageenan-based film, the film's homogeneity and smooth surface are unaffected, indicating the creation of hydrogen bonds and electrostatic interactions between the surface groups of carrageenan and the hydroxyl groups of extract and CDs (Riahi et al., 2024a).

The thermal charactrazation of CS-based composite films was assessed using TGA and DTG analysis, as illustrated in Fig. 4B. All films indicated four distinct thermal degradation patterns during heated, ranging from 25 °C to 600 °C (Fig. 4 Ba). The first phase of weight loss occurred in the first step (25–150 °C) can be ascribed to the moisture evaporation physically present via hydrogen bonding with the -OH group of CS/SPI polymer chain and glycerol within the film matrix. The next thermal degradation (150 °C–220 °C) was correlated with the degradation of glycerol as a plasticizer. Pure CS film experienced higher weight loss than other blended films, indicating it had poor thermal attributes (Xing, Zhang, Li, Li, & Shi, 2018). The gradual decrease in weight loss was recorded after adding SPI, suggesting that SPI improved the thermal stability of the pure CS film (K. Li et al., 2017; Xing et al., 2018). All films with CDs and/or anthocyanins had hydrophilic nature, which adsorb water molecules via physical adsorption, result in a slower therm decompostion rate in this phase of the process. Indeed, the peak temperature of treated films was slightly higher than pure CS or CS/SPI films, which can be correlated with the enhanced interaction via hydrogen bonding between hydroxyl groups of the polymer backbone and the hydrophil CDs/extract (Sangeetha et al., 2024; Yang et al., 2024). The major degradation of the films that happened between 220 °C and 320 °C, which can be related to the decomposition of the polymer backbone and carbon-based structure (Koshy et al., 2022; Sangeetha et al., 2024). In the fourth phase, from 320 °C to 520 °C, weight loss appeared to gradually decelerate, which attributed to the carbonized polymers in film matrix. In similar study, Sul et al. (2023) reported that the incorporation of banana peel CDs increased the thermal stability of CS/gelatin-based functional films due to the formation of potent intermolecular forces between incorporated dots and biopolymer matrix (Sul et al., 2023). Zhan et al. (2024)also found that adding red cabbage anthocyanins enhanced the gellan gum-based film's thermal stability (Yang et al., 2024). According to the DTG curve (Fig. 4Bb), in the temperature ranging from 220 °C to 320 °C, where most thermal degradation happened, the incorporation of the dots/anthocyanins decreased the weight lose. Meanwhile, the lowest stability and most weight lose was observed in pure CS film which closely ascribed to a complex process involving the dehydration of saccharide rings, depolymerization, and the breakdown of both acetylated and deacetylated units in the polymers (Xing et al., 2018). Previous studies have indicated that dots/anthocyanins possess higher thermal stability than biopolymers (Sani, Tavassoli, Salim, Azizi-lalabadi, & McClements, 2022; Sul et al., 2023; Yang et al., 2024), which may account for this observation.

The films' thickness varied from 69.00 μm to 81.60 μm for the for pure CS and CS/SPI/PHPA/PHP-CDs films, respectively (Fig. 5 A). The increase in the composite film's thickness resulted from the incorporation of solid-dense materials (Khezerlou et al., 2023; Sul et al., 2023).

The wettability and hydrophobicity/hydrophilicity of the films are mainly done by using water contact angle (WCA) measurement (Fig. 5B). Biopolymer films are deemed hydrophilic if the contact angle is below 65°. The WCA of the pure CS film was 56.3°, showing that the CS film has a hydrophilic surface. The WCA of CS/SPI films further decreased compared with pure CS film, showing the deposition of SPI-endowed composite film's hydrophilic trend. The addition of PHP-CDs, PHPA, and PHP-CDs/PHPA to the CS/SPI matrix led to a significant (p < 0.05) increase in the WCA to 72.3°, 70.1°, and 86.3°, respectively, resulting in augmented surface hydrophobicity, owing to the enhanced synergistic cross-linking, the films exhibited increased firmness, as evident from the morphology analysis. In other word, reducing hydrophilicity could be attributed to the decrease in available hydrophilic free hydroxyl groups as a result of the hydrogen bonding between anthocyanins and dots and their interactions with the biopolymer matrix (Wagh et al., 2023). The reason for CS/SPI/PHPA film's less WCA than CS/SPI/PHP-CDs film might be related to the enhancement of surface roughness and hydrophobicity introduced by CDs. A similar result was also found for carrageenan and cellulose nanofiber-based films incorporated with CDs and extracts derived from sweet potato peels (Riahi et al., 2024a) and Brassica oleracea (Wagh et al., 2023), respectively.

The WVP, MC, SI, and WS of pure CS films are exhibited in Fig. 5C–F and are measured to be 3.10 × 10−11 g m/m2 s. Pa, 98.00 %, 15.60 %, and 69.20 %, respectively. The addition of SPI resulted in a significant decrease in the WVP, MC, and SI of the blended films (P < 0.05), recorded as 2.90 × 10−11 g m/m2 s. Pa, 96.00 %, and 14.30 %, respectively, which this can result from the rise in solid content and the displacement of some water molecules within the film's pore structure by SPI. However, the film solubility increased to 72.3 % after adding SPI. This can be due to the relatively higher aqueous solubility of SPI compared to CS, as acidic conditions to induce aqueous solubility is necessary for CS (Xing et al., 2018; Yong et al., 2023). Compared to the untreated group, WVP, MC, SI, and WS of CS-based films loaded with PHP-CDs, PHPA, and PHP-CDs/PHPA were significantly lower (P < 0.05), which could be correlated with enhancement of hydrophobicity introduced by CDs within the films as well as raising hydrophobic interaction between non-polar components in PHPA and CS/SPI polymer. In a similar study, Wagh et al. (2023) found that adding CDs and extract from Brassica oleracea into the cellulose nanofiber-based films caused a significant decrease in the WVP (7.81 → 5.27 × 10−9 g m/m2.Pa) of the prepared films compared to control group (Wagh et al., 2023).

To safeguard packaged food products during supply chain, packaging systems should possess sufficient mechanical strength and integrity to prevent damage or leakage (Khezerlou, Zolfaghari, et al., 2023). The mechanical properties of the CS-based films are illustrated in Fig. 5G. The pure CS films are relatively firm and stiff with 65.35 ± 0.12 MPa for TS and, but less flexible with EAB of 13.25 ± 0.02 %, signifying its rigid and fragile characteristics. CS's firm six-membered ring structure, internal hydrogen bonds, and ionic side groups and polar intermolecular interaction of CS molecules limits molecular movement, enhancing stiffness and rigidity in CS films (Xing et al., 2018). It turned out that the TS for CS/SPI films is higher than that of pure CS film, while the EAB slightly decreased, which was attributed to the enhancement of interaction and hydrogen bonds between CS and SPI that decrease the flexibility and processability of the blended film (the lowest among the five films) (Yong et al., 2023). After adding PHP-CDs, PHPA, and PHP-CDs/PHPA, the TS of the all treated CS/SPI film decreased significantly (p < 0.05), but the EB increased significantly (p < 0.05). This trend can be attributed to nanosized dots filling the spaces between CS and SPI polymer chains, reducing intermolecular forces. The dots likely facilitate easier movement of polymer chains through a plasticizing effect on CS/SPI matrix. Further, anthocyanins serve as plasticizers, enhancing molecular mobility within the film matrix and improving its flexibility (Riahi et al., 2024a). Other studies gained similar results (Riahi et al., 2024a), where dots/anthocyanin derived from Kohlrabi peel decreases the film strength but significantly increase the flexibility of carrageenan-based packaging films.

The transparency results of the CS-based films can be viewed in Fig. 5H. The pure CS and CS/SPI films had the highest transparency (∼27.42 and ∼25.68). The incorporation of PHP-CDs and PHPA to these films significantly (P < 0.05) decreased their transparency (∼24.76 and ∼23.41, respectively), which may be caused by the selective scattering of light by the nanosized CDs nanoparticles. Further, the chromophore groups in the anthocyanins absorb light waves (Khezerlou et al., 2023). These findings were in line with the previous research, which indicated that the incorporation of TiO2 and anthocyanins into the gelatin/κ-carrageenan films significantly decreased the film's transparency (Sani et al., 2022).

The antioxidant properties of CS-based films were assessed using DPPH and ABTS radical solutions across six different-sized samples, with the results illustrated in Fig. 6 A and B. Interestingly, an increase in the film size corresponded to a rise in its antioxidant activity. Additionally, the introduction of CDs significantly enhanced the antioxidant activity of the films, and the inclusion of PHPA had a synergistic effect, further enhancing the antioxidant properties of the CS-based films. Notably, the CS/SPI/PHPA/PHP-CDs film displayed the highest antioxidant properties across all sample sizes. The varying extent of radical inhibition against ABTS and DPPH can be attributed to the rapid release of CDs in the ABTS aqueous solution compared to the DPPH methanolic solution. These findings indicate that CDs maintain their antioxidant effectiveness even after being incorporated into the composite, with their effectiveness increasing in proportion to the film size. Compared to the other films, CS (CS/SPI) films indicated the lowest antioxidant activity with 3.8 ± 0.002 % (4.26 ± 0.004 %) and 4.23 ± 0.003 % (4.56 ± 0.002 %) of DPPH• and ABTS•+ scavenging activity, respectively, which primarily attributed to the influence of the hydroxyl group at C-3 and the amino group at C-2 in CS (Muthu et al., 2021; Yong et al., 2023). The fortification of the CS/SPI matrix with antioxidant-rich PHP-CDs, PHPA, and PHP-CDs/PHPA (Fig. 6A) indicated high antioxidant activity, with 70.2 ± 0.09 %, 76.13 ± 0.089 %, and 82.3 ± 0.1 % of DPPH• radical scavenging activity, respectively. Meanwhile, the antioxidant activity of CDs reached 72.3 ± 0.078 %, 78.36 ± 0.11 %, and 90.6 ± 0.1 % for ABTS, respectively, which higher than DPPH (Fig. 6B). Abundant phenolic hydroxyl groups are possessed by anthocyanin, which are rich in electrons, allowing anthocyanin to easily donate electrons and hydrogen atoms to reactive free radicals, effectively neutralizing them (Riahi et al., 2024a). Earlier research has also indicated that the antioxidant activity of pistachio hull mainly ascribed to gallic acid, catechin, epicatechin, eriodictyol-7-O-glucoside, and cyanidin-3-O-galactoside (Belyagoubi-Benhammou et al., 2024; Elhadef et al., 2021). It is believed that the improved antioxidant potential of the CS/SPI films loaded with CDs is closely attributed to the functional groups (such as hydroxyl and carboxyl groups) present on the surface of PHP-CDs. Protonating CD's functional groups can reduce and quench the scavenging effect of DPPH• and ABTS by forming DPPH-H and ABTS-H, respectively. Further, by generating adducts through the sp2 carbon core, CDs play an undeniable role in neutralizing free radicals, leading to spin delocalization along the conjugated CD's backbone (Khan et al., 2023; Riahi et al., 2024a). Notably, the discrepancies in antioxidant performances of PHP-CDs with PHP-CDs-rich CS/SPI films can be explained by the physical interactions and chemical interactions between PHP-CDs and the chitosan and SPI chain in the film matrix (Y. Li et al., 2023).

The antibacterial effect of CS-based films against E. coli and S. aureus is illustrated in Fig. 6C. The pure CS and CS/SPI films indicated a weak antibacterial effect against both tested bacteria, which is attributed to the antibacterial ability of CS (Muthu et al., 2021; Yong et al., 2023). In contrast, the incorporation of PHPA, PHP-CDs, and PHP-CDs/PHPA into CS-based films exhibited significant antibacterial activity against E. coli (S. aureus) with inhibition zone of 14.3 ± 0.12 mm (14.89 ± 0.2 mm), 17.36 ± 0.1 mm (18.2 ± 0.3 mm), and 20.36 ± 0.21 mm (22.1 ± 36±0.24 mm), respectively. The enhanced antibacterial potential of the CD-loaded film mainly stems from the antimicrobial abilities of PHP-CDs. Meanwhile, cell's intracellular proteins can be influenced by active PHPA, leading to the inhibition of bacterial reproduction and growth, which can be attributed to the abundant antimicrobial phenolic groups in pistachio hull extract (Belyagoubi-Benhammou et al., 2024; Elhadef et al., 2021). It is important to mention that the CS-based film exhibited a lower bactericidal rate against E. coli in comparison to S. aureus. Several research has noticed that the simultaneous incorporation of extracts and CDs derived natural-based plant materials into different biopolymers-based packaging films, such as cellulose nanofiber (Wagh et al., 2023), carrageenan (Riahi et al., 2024a), Riahi et al., 2024bnd CS/gelatin films (Sul et al., 2023), imparts significant antioxidant and antibacterial activities. The addition of PHP-CDs and/or PHPA is believed to safeguard the prepared films from being contaminated by foodborne pathogens, thereby preserving their antimicrobial abilities throughout the food packaging duration. Regarding antimicrobial effects, free CDs indicated a certain level of antibacterial efficacy, however, when integrated into chitosan-based films, this ability is slightly improved. The CS/SPI film matrix may facilitate sustained release and increased interaction with target pathogens because of its structural properties, improving the overall antibacterial activity (Y. Li et al., 2023; Yang et al., 2024). Further, this enhanced antibacterial activity can be attributed to the synergistic effect of the chitosan, anthocyanins, and CDs in the film matrix.

In this study, 10% (v/v) and 95% (v/v) ethanol solutions were utilized as substitutes for alcoholic and fatty foods to assess the release behavior of PHPA and PHPA/PHP-CD. Fig. 6D demonstrates that in both food simulant environments (10% and 95% ethanol), the release trend of PHPA is higher when applied alone to CS/SPI film compared to when added to PHPA/PHP-CD. The highest release in 10% ethanol was observed in CS/SPI/PHPA film, indicating that the addition of PHPA extracts alone resulted in significant release, possibly due to the interaction of hydroxyl groups in anthocyanin and water molecules (Tavassoli, Khezerlou, Sani, et al., 2024), with equilibrium reached in 90 min. Conversely, the film of PHPA combined with PHP-CDs showed less release in both conditions, with the release rate in equilibrium in the 95% ethanol solution. Notably, the polarity of the simulants is related to the higher release rate of PHPA in 10% ethanol than in 95% ethanol. These findings suggest that PHPA is effectively incorporated into the structure of PHP-CD, providing the film packaging material with a long and controlled release effect. Here, release test findings explain the difference in antioxidant ability between DPPH and ABTS methods of CS-based films. Similar to our findings, Riahi, Rhim, Bagheri, Pircheraghi, and Lotfali (2022) observed that the release of CS-based CDs from carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC)-based films in various solutions showed an initial rapid release followed by a plateau, with the peak rate in a 10% alcohol solution. They reported that this was related to the hydrophilicity of CDs and the limited swelling of CMC films in alcohol (Riahi et al., 2022). Ezati, Rhim, Molaei, Priyadarshi, and Han (2022) also found that glucose-based CDs (GCD) release rapidly in water and slower in alcohol, with reduced rates in higher alcohol concentrations. They noted that the release rates were significantly influenced by the solubility of the polymer matrix and the affinity between the solvent and GCD (Ezati et al., 2022).

The UV–visible transmittance/absorbance can reflect the light-blocking properties of the films. Fig. 7 illustrates the optical transmittance behavior of the CS/SPI-based composite films across a wavelength range of 200–800 nm. The pure CS film had no color and was very transparent. The highest transmission and minimal absorption of ultraviolet rays in the pure CS film can be attributed to its uniform network structure and the low concentration of UV-absorbing groups present in this polymer type (Alizadeh Sani, Khezerlou, Tavassoli, Abedini, & McClements, 2024). The incorporation of SPI decreased the transmission and rose the absorption, which this can be related to phase separation of the biopolymer mixtures after blending. Indeed, as a result of minor thermodynamic incompatibility, various microscopic domains formed within the films, causing light scattering (Sani et al., 2022). After adding PHPA, the film had a pale peach color but was still transparent. Incorporating PHP-CDs alters the film's hue to a reddish-purple shade due to the combined impact of SPA's purple hue and the yellowish-brown color of PHP-CDs. The PHP-CDs/PHPA-rich film exhibited a smooth coloration, showing an even dispersion of anthocyanin and dots throughout the polymer matrix. The incorporation of PHP-CDs or PHPA decreased the film's transmittance and augmented its absorbance, which can be attributed to the absorption of light waves by the chromophore groups (like C=C and C=O) in the aromatic ring of PHPA (Cao et al., 2023; Tavassoli, Khezerlou, Bakhshizadeh, et al., 2024) as well as the enhanced light scattering ability by CDs as well as large surface area of these ultra-small nanoparticles (size; 4 nm), leading to a darker color (Riahi et al., 2024a; Riahi et al., 2022). Especially noteworthy is the high absorbance and low transmittance exhibited by the films within the 200–330 nm range, potentially providing a protective barrier for UV-sensitive food products prone to degradation when exposed to ultraviolet light. Other studies have also confirmed that incorporating various kinds of CDs or anthocyanins into biopolymer matrices can decrease the transmission of UV–visible light (Cao et al., 2023; Riahi et al., 2024b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Response of CS-based films to pH variation
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 8 and Table 2 illustrates that the pH-responsive CS/SPI-based films exhibited a satisfactory color response at different pH levels (pH 2–12) consistent with the outcomes of immersing PHPA extract in buffer solutions. The visual pH-stimuli color response of CS/SPI/PHP-CDs/PHPA and as follows: pH 2 = pale peach, pH 4–6 = light magenta, pH 7 = light amber hue, pH 8 = khaki, pH 10 = yellowish-brown, and pH 12 = brown. Riahi et al., (2024) also found that the color of the carrageenan-based film containing anthocyanin and CDs derived from sweet potato peels changed from reddish-purple to blue/green as the pH was increased from 2 to 12, respectively (Riahi et al., 2024a). The corresponding instrumental color variations, including L, a, and b of the CS/SPI/PHPA/PHP-CDs films are shown in Fig. 8A. The L∗ (lightness; 51.98 → 36.23) and a∗ (greenness/redness; 4.84 → 23.1) of films indicated a significant (P < 0.05) downward trend from acidic to alkaline pH values, showing the high intensity of redness and greenness of the films at highly acidic and basic conditions, respectively (Heydarian et al., 2023). While the b∗ (blueness/yellowness; 6.49 → 30.25) significantly (P < 0.05) increased with raising pH from 2 to 12, respectively, showed that the color of films was changed to brown (Heydarian et al., 2023; Shavisi, 2024). The pH-responsive color charge of films is closely correlated with the type and structure of the anthocyanins. There are similar reports about decreasing the L∗ and a∗ as well as increasing b∗ of pH-sensitive colorimetric films after the incorporation of natural dyes, such as sweet potato peels (Riahi et al., 2024a), Riahi et al., 2024black barberry (Heydarian et al., 2023), red poppy (Tavassoli, Khezerlou, Bakhshizadeh, et al., 2024), and Malva sylvestris (Shavisi, 2024).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Response of CS-based films to ammonia vapor
ce:section.ce:para: The ammonia sensitivity of the films was assessed to mimic the developed colorimetric films' response to volatile nitrogenous compounds formed during spoilage of flesh food sample. The color sensitivity of CS/SPI/PHP-CDs/PHPA films to volatile NH3 are illustrated in Fig. 8B. After 30 min exposure to NH3, the colorimetric films showed obvious color changes from 0% initially to 81.2% after 30 min. The sensitivity of the colorimetric films to ammonia vapor increased significantly (P < 0.05) with enhancing exposure time as follows: 5 min; 46.3%, 10 min; 69.53%, 15 min; 72.1%, 20 min; 74.2%, 25 min; 79.6%, and 30 min; 81.2%. The alteration in the color of the prepared films may be associated with the interplay between the phenolic hydroxyl groups of PHPA, hydrolyzed hydroxyl ions (OH−), the formation of hydrated ammonia, and the generation of phenolic oxygen anions (Riahi et al., 2024a; Shavisi, 2024). The rapid and obvious color response exhibited by the developed films, as shown in Fig. 8B, makes them well-suited for use in intelligent packaging systems. Similarly, Kim research team (2024) found that the pink carrageenan-sweet potato anthocyanins composite film turned green/yellowish after the exposure to volatile NH3 within 15 min (Riahi et al., 2024a). Heydarian and Shavisi et al. (2023also reported that white gelatin-xanthan gum nanofibers containing black barberry anthocyanins shifted to violet, green, and brown after 4, 12, and 20 min exposure to NH3, respectively (Heydarian et al., 2023).

During storage, seafood and flesh food products are highly susceptible to microbial infections and release TVB-N, resulting in pH and color alterations in intelligent packaging. The pH value plays a crucial role in determining the seafood freshness due to its strong correlation with microbial growth and food spoilage. Hence, monitoring and controlling the pH level is an important factor in food preservation. From this perspective, in first scenario, CS/SPI/PHPA/PHP-CDs film were used as colorimetric pH indicators to track the fish quality under 4 °C/12 d and 25 °C/8 d and the changing color of the prepared films is shown in Fig. 9 A and C. Also, Fig. 9B illustrated the microbial changes (i.e., TVC and PTC) and changes of pH, PV, and TVB-N of fish samples during storage. The initial TVC, PTC, pH, PV, and TVB-N of fresh fish fillets in 25 °C were 3.3 ± 0.6 log CFU/g, 2.1 ± 0.2 log CFU/g, 6.2 ± 0.1, 0.5 ± 0.03 meq peroxide/kg fat, and 6.8 ± 0.9 mg N/100 g, respectively, affirming the appropriate quality of the fish fillets. Raw fish fillets with TVC, PTC, pH, PV, and TVB-N higher than 7 log CFU/g, 7 log CFU/g, 7, 1 meq peroxide/kg fat, and 25 mg N/100 g, respectively, is considered as unsuitable food with poor quality (Ramezani, Zarei, & Raminnejad, 2015). Accordingly, with passing storage time at 25 °C (4 °C), the fish samples spoiled after 2 d (5 d) of storage (Fig. 9C). Comparable outcomes were noted for fillets of silver carp (Shavisi, 2024) and rainbow trout (Hematian, Baghaei, Mohammadi Nafchi, & Bolandi, 2023)during storage at 4 °C and 25 °C, respectively. At the end of storage time at 25 °C (4 °C), TVC, PTC, pH, PV, and TVB-N finally reached 7.38 ± 0.25 log CFU/g (6.89 ± 0.13 log CFU/g), 4.6 ± 0.14 log CFU/g (4.1 ± 0.16 log CFU/g), 8.7 ± 0.12 (8.49 ± 1.2), 1.79 ± 0.01 meq peroxide/kg fat (1.62 ± 0.03 meq peroxide/kg fat), and 53.9 ± 1.2 mg N/100 g (38.3 ± 1.1 mg N/100 g), respectively. Meanwhile, as seen in Fig. 9A, the resultant increase in pH inside of the package resulted in a visible color variation at 4 °C and 25 °C; light lylac (first 5d and 2 d) → pale pink (7 d and 3 d) → light yellow (9 d and 5 d) → finally brown (11 d–12 d and 7 d). The brownish-yellow hue of the CS/SPI/PHP-CDs/PHPA film signifies that the fish fillets has deteriorated and is no longer safe for consumption. The color shift in prepared films could be linked to the increasing pH level and the generation of volatile alkali gases resulting from the decomposition of fish proteins (Hematian et al., 2023; Wagh et al., 2023). Nassim Shavisi (2024) reported that the initial TVC (3.44 log CFU/g), PTC (2.88 log CFU/g), pH (6.54), and TVB-N (1.43 mg N/100 g) of silver carp fillets increased and reached 8.99 log CFU/g, 8.11 log CFU/g, 8.24, and 40.23 mg N/100 g during 8 d of storage, respectively (Shavisi, 2024). Meanwhile, the color of Malva sylvestris extract + chitosan/carrageenan mats as a pH-stimuli indicator shifted from initial white to dark blue (4 d), and finally green (8 d). Cao et al. (2023) also found that the pH and TVB-N of spoiled pork on 6 d (32 h) were 6.28 (6.01) and 16.1 mg N/100 g (16.2 mg N/100 g) at 4 °C (20 °C), respectively, and the color of anthocyanin-rich sodium alginate/chitosan films changed from red to blue.

As given in Fig. 9B, the initial days of storage at 4 °C (25 °C), the initial redness of the films with L∗; 65.98, a∗; 16.64, and b∗; 20.98 parameters shows the suitable quality of the fish fillets. At the end of storage time, the L∗, a∗, and b∗ values of film reached 35.87, 13.25, and 30.49 with chemical and microbial spoilage of fish fillets after 12 d, respectively, and corresponding color parameters at 25 °C were concurrently recorded (L∗; 45.78, a∗; 13.47, and b∗; 40.63). Further, the film's ΔE value exceeded 5 during storage, suggesting that even unskilled panelists could easily detect the color alteration and fish quality status. Goudarzi, Moshtaghi, and Shahbazi (2023) also indicated that the L∗ (96.87) and a∗ (−2.25) parameters of Prunus domestica extract + PVA/κ-carrageena nanofiber mats diminished and reached 51.19 and −12.44 with chemical spoilage of minced lamb meat after 15 d, respectively, parallel to it the prepared mats indicated a visible color changes from white to purplish-red (Goudarzi et al., 2023). Chen, Zeng, et al. (2023) also observed a transition in the color of a gelatin-based film loaded with plum peel extract from red and orange-pink to light green over a storage period of 10 d (K. Chen, Zeng, et al., 2023). Correspondingly, the pH increased from 5.82 to 6.84, and the TVB-N level in fresh chicken increased from 7.73 mg/100 g to 34.58 mg/100 g.

The susceptibility of fish to microbial and chemical infections poses a significant challenge in maintaining their quality during storage (Z. Li et al., 2024; Shavisi, 2024). The TVC and PTC are the key indicators for evaluating the freshness and overall quality of fish samples stored under cold aerobic storage conditions, consequently, the enumeration of TVC and PTC can provide valuable insights into the quality of chilled fish samples (Z. Li et al., 2024). The formation of TVB-N compounds can induce changes in pH levels and alter the natural coloration of the fish samples, factors that can be monitored through advanced intelligent packaging systems (Bakhshizadeh et al., 2023; Khezerlou et al., 2023). By determining the PV, one can effectively monitor the level of oxidative deterioration in fish samples (Ghalehjooghi, Tajik, & Shahbazi, 2023; Z. Li et al., 2024). Therefore, we assessed the appearance and chemical/microbial quality changes of fish fillets in second scenario. From this perspectives, a packaging trail was performed to evaluate the efficacy of the CD-and anthocyanins-added CS/SPI film for active packaging application.

Fig. 10 B-E presents the microbiological analysis (i.e., TVC and PTC) and chemical properties (i.e., pH, PV, TBA, and TVB-N) in fish fillets packaged with the untreated group, control group (neat CS/SPI film), and treated group (CS/SPI/PHPA/PHP-CDs film). Raw fish is generally a favorable medium for microbial growth. The initial TVC, and PTC of fresh fish samples were determined to be 2.1 ± 0.09 and 3.1 ± 0.23 log CFU/g, respectively (Fig. 10 C and D), suggesting a standard quality and safety for the samples. Fish is considered unsuitable for human consumption when the TVC and PTC exceeds 7 Log CFU/g. As anticipated, within the PE film packaging group, the TVC and PTC in the fish exceeded acceptable threshold and increased steadily to 3.65 ± 0.26 Log CFU/mL and 2.4 ± 0.04 Log CFU/mL after 4 d of storage, respectively, as PE film presented no antibacterial potential. However, in the control group (fish packaged with pure CS/SPI film), the TVC and PTC reached 6.8 ± 0.36 Log CFU/mL and 4.3 ± 0.3 Log CFU/mL on the same day, respectively, and exceeded the acceptable threshold levels on 7th d, showing that the CS/SPI film indicates a bacteriostatic effects owing to the antibacterial function of CS as recorded in the antibacterial ability study of the film. The final TVC and PTC of samples wrapped with treated group films were in the ranges of 5.8 ± 0.13 Log CFU/mL and 3.65 ± 0.16 Log CFU/mL at the end of storage period, respectively. This affirmed that PHP-CDs/PHPA-rich film provoked the best protective role against microbial growth under cold aerobic storage conditions of fish fillets compared with other groups. The antimicrobial function of the CS/SPI/PHP-CDs/PHPA film is mainly attributed to the combined antimicrobial abilities of CS, extracts, and dots. Sul et al. (2023) suggested that the CS/gelatin film enriched with CDs could retard the increase in TVC (2.2 → 3.5 Log CFU/mL) in after 2 d of storage of meat sample compared with the control samples (2.2 → 7.2 Log CFU/mL) (Sul et al., 2023). Further similar findings were reported for silver carp fillets treated with probiotic + gelatin-sodium alginate nanofibers (Ghalehjooghi et al., 2023), pork wrapped in banana peel CDs + CS/gelatin film (Sul et al., 2023), and minced beef packaged with gelatin/PVA + CDs/grapefruit seed extract film (Min, Ezati, Yoon, & Rhim, 2023).

The changes of pH, PV, TBA, and TVB-N of fish fillets under cold storage for 12 d are shown in Fig. 10. The initial pH, PV, TBA, and TVB-N of fish fillets were measured to be 5.98 ± 0.1, 0.4 ± 0.03 meq peroxide/1000 g, 0.19 ± 0.02 mg MDA/kg lipid, and 5.89 ± 0.3 mg N/100 g, respectively (Fig. 10B,C and E). Fish was considered as fresh when PV and TBA was below 5 meq peroxide/1000 g and 1 mg MDA/kg, respectively. As depicted in Fig. 10 C and E, the PV and TBA values for fish fillets in both the PE package and treated film package initially followed a similar pattern (after 2 d). This can be ascribed to the effective hydrophobic and gas barrier properties of the PE film, which partially restricted the entry of O2 molecules. The PV and TBA of PE film and packaged samples with PHP-CDs/PHPA-rich film significantly (P < 0.05) increased during storage period and were determined to be 0.82 ± 0.03 meq peroxide/1000 g and 0.56 ± 0.023 mg MDA/kg at the end of the study, respectively. The lower PV and TBRA levels of treated fish samples compared to the unpackaged fillets was mainly attributed to the presence of antioxidant anthocyanins and dots in the prepared film that delayed the degree of primary and secondary lipid oxidation in fish samples (Min et al., 2023; Riahi et al., 2024a; Riahi et al., 2022). These results in line with those found for adding zinc-doped CDs-derived avocado peel into cellulose nanofibers (CNF)/pullulan films for extending the shelf life of chicken and tofu (Riahi et al., 2024b). In another study, the TBA value of pork meat wrapped in the coffee grounds CD-incorporated CNF films remained below 0.4 mg MDA/kg lipid after 21 d, lower than the control group by 60–67% (Sul, Khan, & Rhim, 2024).

pH and TVB-N levels of PE film group and packaged fillets with pure CS/SPI film reached the maximum acceptable level (pH; 7 and TVB-N; 25 mg N/100 g (Shavisi, 2024)) on 4th and 6th d of cold storage conditions, respectively (Fig. 10B). However, PHP-CDs/PHPA-rich film maintained pH and TVB-N levels within the limit for more than 10 d (pH; 6.97 ± 0.1 and TVB-N; 7.6 ± 0.9 mg N/100 g), which this mainly attributed to high ability of CS, PHP-CDs, and PHPA in preventing microorganism's spoilage, lipid oxidation, and O2 molecules penetration (Min et al., 2023; Riahi et al., 2024a; Riahi et al., 2022).

Multifunctional active and intelligent indicator films were created using CDs and anthocyanins extracted from PHP. These were found to be highly compatible with the Cs/SPI-based film, and their addition improved the film's physical, mechanical, and microstructure properties. The CS/SPI/PHPA/PHP-CDs film showed remarkable properties such as antioxidant, antibacterial, and UV-blocking, making it an ideal packaging material. The film's visual color response to pH buffers and volatile ammonia was tested, and it was found to be highly sensitive, showing a continuous color change from light pink to yellowish-brown. This change can be easily observed with the naked eye and was compatible during the storage of fish samples, accompanied by levels of pH, TVB-N, TVC, PV, PTC, and TBA. Finally, the films of CS/SPI/PHPA/PHP-CDs, as intelligent and active materials, possess the ability to enhance the shelf life of food products and enable consumers to visually identify the quality of food items stored under refrigerated and room-temperature conditions. These films are considered promising materials for the food packaging industry as they have the potential to enhance food safety and quality while also reducing food waste.

More comprehemsive reserch should focus on comparing this innovative approach to traditional packaging systems, highlighting its benefits and Strengths in sustainability and waste reduction. There is also a critical need to explore the best utilization of pistachio by-products, maximizing its effectiiveness in industrial applications. More so, exploring the scalability of utilizing agricultural by-products could further support the SDGs, particularly in promoting responsible consumption and production (SDG 12) and ensuring sustainable food systems (SDG 2). Increased research in this area can significantly elevate the value of agricultural waste and foster a circular economy within the food packaging industry.
